coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance criteria completeness and verifiability will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and verifiability

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `grep -qi "sidecar fallback" docs/plan-review-gate.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit `python -m pytest` oracle commands to AC2 and AC3, e.g. `python -m pytest tests/unit -k "remediation" -x` (same filter, since negative-case and signature-parity tests belong in the same module and the filter already covers them), so all six ACs have a concrete, runnable proving command.
evidence: AC2 oracle: 'negative-case unit tests in the same module covering both.' (no command)
AC3 oracle: 'unit test asserting the signature-path result is unchanged for a signed fixture.' (no command)
AC1 oracle by contrast: '`python -m pytest tests/unit -k "remediation" -x` green' — sets the pattern that ACs 2 and 3 fail to follow
findings.finding: AC2 and AC3 lack concrete runnable oracle commands. AC2 says 'Oracle: negative-case unit tests in the same module covering both' and AC3 says 'Oracle: unit test asserting the signature-path result is unchanged for a signed fixture' — both describe what the tests do but provide no invocable pytest command, unlike AC1 which includes an explicit `python -m pytest ...` invocation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 and AC3
scenarios: An executing agent cannot mechanically verify AC2 or AC3 is met without inferring the command from AC1's pattern — a gap that silently allows partial coverage (e.g. the negative-case tests could be skipped and CI still reports green on AC1's narrower `-k` filter).
findings.suggested_fix: AC2 oracle: append `; python -m pytest tests/unit -k "remediation" -x` green (the negative-case tests live in the same module, so the existing filter exercises them). AC3 oracle: append `; python -m pytest tests/unit -k "remediation" -x` green (the signed-fixture regression test also lives in the same module).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC-1/AC-3) asserting that `remediation_mode_candidate` returns a dict containing `baseline == "sidecar"` on the fallback path and `baseline == "signature"` on the signature path.
evidence: "the returned dict gains `baseline: \"signature\" | \"sidecar\"`" — What section
No AC text references the `baseline` key or asserts its value for either path
findings.finding: The What section describes the returned decision dict gaining a new `baseline: "signature" | "sidecar"` field, but no AC verifies this field is present or has the correct value in either branch — it is described work with no covering criterion.
findings.location: What section (decision-dict shape per branch); Acceptance Criteria (all six ACs)
scenarios: An implementer could omit `baseline` from the returned dict entirely and all six ACs would still pass, leaving the described shape unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC-1's oracle to assert `result['baseline'] == 'sidecar'` and AC-3's oracle to assert `result['baseline'] == 'signature'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (or extend existing ones) covering: (a) when signature verification errors occur, the sidecar fallback is consulted; (b) when a valid signature exists but its manifest is not a plan-review manifest, the sidecar fallback is consulted.
evidence: "BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline - a signature verification error AND a successful verification whose manifest is not a plan-review manifest (the early return at attest.py:231) - now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible" — What section
AC-3 only tests the positive signature path ('With a valid certified signature present'); no AC tests behavior when verification errors or a non-plan-review manifest is encountered.
findings.finding: The What section specifies that two previously-ineligible paths (a signature verification error AND a successful verification of a non-plan-review manifest) now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible; no AC covers these two new fall-through cases.
findings.location: What section ('BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline … now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible'); Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: An implementer keeps the old early-return for the non-plan-review-manifest case (since no test catches it), and BLOCK loops using that path remain structurally ineligible — the primary bug is not fully fixed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two negative-signature unit tests to the same module as AC-1/AC-2: one injecting a verification error, one injecting a non-plan-review-manifest, both asserting `result['baseline'] == 'sidecar'` and that eligibility is determined by sidecar fields.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the fall-through behavior change (e.g., a feature flag to restore the old ineligible return for the verification-error and non-plan-review-manifest paths, or a documented one-line revert procedure).
evidence: "BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline … now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible" — What section
"all other fail-safe behavior (any read error -> ineligible -> full review) is preserved" — What section (acknowledges the change but provides only partial fail-safe, not a rollback path)
No mention of a feature flag, canary rollout, or back-out procedure anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan changes the behavior of two previously-ineligible code paths (verification error, non-plan-review-manifest) to now consult the sidecar, which can grant eligibility — a behavioral change to existing paths — but provides no explicit rollback or back-out mechanism if the sidecar incorrectly grants eligibility in production.
findings.location: What section ('BOTH paths … now fall through to the sidecar branch'); Why section; Scope
scenarios: If the sidecar fallback incorrectly grants eligibility (e.g., stale sidecar data, edge case in None handling) in the verification-error path, the rising floor engages when it should not; there is no documented way to revert to the previous always-ineligible behavior for these paths without a code rollback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an internal architectural note (ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or a sidecar-schema design-doc section) capturing the sidecar's new dual role, the three invariants that emit must always satisfy (baseline fields self-sourced, SHA two-step resolution, additive-optional schema), and the contract that binds sidecar.py to attest.py's fallback branch.
evidence: Plan promotes sidecar to carry 'verified_at_sha' and 'regver' as baseline fields always self-sourced at emit time
Plan states these are 'additive-optional on the existing plan-review sidecar schema' but schema invariants are only described in the What prose, not in any named design doc for sidecar maintainers
docs/plan-review-gate.md update is scoped to eligibility behavior (user-facing), not the internal contract between sidecar emission and eligibility evaluation
No ADR or CLAUDE.md update is mentioned
findings.finding: The plan promotes the sidecar from a result-record to an eligibility-baseline-persistence mechanism — a dual-role architectural contract that binds sidecar.py and attest.py going forward — but captures this decision only in the user-facing docs/plan-review-gate.md eligibility paragraph. Future maintainers of either module have no internal note (ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or sidecar schema doc) explaining the invariants they must preserve: (1) the three baseline fields must be stamped at emit time, not derived from verdict contents, (2) both sides of code_unchanged must use the same two-step SHA resolution, (3) the fallback branch must never be consulted when a valid manifest exists. Without this, the coupling is invisible to someone refactoring sidecar.py or adding a new emit call path.
findings.location: What section (sidecar dual-role) and Scope/AC4; docs/plan-review-gate.md update
scenarios: A future engineer adds a new sidecar emit call path (e.g., for a new verdict type) and omits the two new fields — the fallback silently degrades to fail-safe without a clear contract violation
A refactor of sidecar.py changes how verified_at_sha is resolved without realising attest.py depends on the specific two-step SHA resolution rule
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief section to CLAUDE.md (or a new docs/adr/sidecar-baseline.md) that names the dual-role contract, lists the three invariants, and cross-references docs/plan-review-gate.md. One paragraph is sufficient; the goal is discoverability for a future sidecar.py maintainer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability instrumentation (at minimum a structured log line at WARNING level) for each new silent-failure branch: subprocess git failure → None, ContextVar miss falling back to git, sidecar fallback branch taken (with reason), and any sidecar field missing that forces a precondition to False.
evidence: 'Sourcing failures stamp None (never raise; emit stays best-effort)' — silent failure with no log
'the returned dict gains baseline: "signature" | "sidecar"' — decision dict records the branch but no log/metric callout
ABSENCE: no mention of log.warning / metric increment / trace span anywhere in the new code paths
'git-HEAD fallback when it is None ... best-effort subprocess, None only if that also fails' — subprocess failure is silently swallowed
findings.finding: The plan adds a new subprocess git-HEAD call and a new sidecar-fallback branch but specifies no logging, metric, or trace instrumentation for these new failure points. When verified_at_sha resolves to None (subprocess failure or ContextVar miss), or when the sidecar fallback branch is taken instead of the signature path, no observable signal is emitted. The decision dict gains a `baseline` key, which surfaces branch selection in the structured payload, but there is no mention of a log line or metric counter for operators to monitor fallback-path adoption rate, subprocess failure rate, or sidecar-field-missing events in production.
findings.location: What section — build_payload / emit changes; remediation_mode_candidate two-branch control flow
scenarios: Subprocess git call fails silently on a CI runner with restricted shell access; verified_at_sha is always None; BLOCK-loop eligibility is always False; operators see no signal to diagnose why the floor never fires.

Sidecar fallback path is never reached due to a latent bug (e.g., sidecar always missing regver); eligible stays False in all BLOCK loops; no metric/log distinguishes this from expected ineligibility.
findings.suggested_fix: In emit/build_payload: log.debug('verified_at_sha resolved via %s', source) where source is 'contextvar'|'git'|'none'. In remediation_mode_candidate: log.debug('remediation baseline: %s', baseline) and log.warning('remediation sidecar missing field %s, precondition False', field_name) for each missing field. Optionally increment a counter metric (e.g., rebar.plan_review.remediation.baseline_source tagged with the branch name) so adoption rate is visible in dashboards.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the git-HEAD fallback in build_payload calls `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` (mapping its `'unknown'` sentinel to `None`) rather than re-implementing the subprocess lookup inline.
evidence: Plan text: 'verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() … with a git-HEAD fallback when it is None (local-mode reads): the committed HEAD SHA of repo_root, best-effort subprocess, None only if that also fails'

signing.py:524-541: head_sha(repo_root) runs subprocess ['git', '-C', str(repo_root), 'rev-parse', 'HEAD'], catches OSError, returns 'unknown' on any failure — byte-identical behavior to what the plan describes
The plan does NOT reference signing.head_sha; it describes the subprocess invocation from scratch as if the helper did not exist
findings.finding: The plan re-describes a new best-effort subprocess git-HEAD SHA lookup inside build_payload (when current_code_sha() is None), but signing.head_sha(repo_root) already encapsulates exactly that logic (subprocess git -C <repo_root> rev-parse HEAD, OSError-safe, returns 'unknown' on failure). Implementing a second subprocess-based HEAD resolver in build_payload duplicates existing codebase functionality.
findings.location: What section — 'verified_at_sha = … with a git-HEAD fallback when it is None … best-effort subprocess, None only if that also fails'
scenarios: Implementer writes a new subprocess git call in build_payload rather than calling signing.head_sha(); now there are two places maintaining git-HEAD resolution logic, with divergent error-handling surface and sentinel values ('unknown' vs None).
If signing.head_sha error handling ever changes (e.g., to handle git worktrees), the sidecar's copy stays stale.
findings.suggested_fix: In build_payload's new repo_root branch: `from rebar import signing; raw = signing.head_sha(repo_root); verified_at_sha = raw if raw != 'unknown' else None`. This reuses the existing helper and avoids a second subprocess pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `sidecar.emit`'s call to `build_payload` (line 60) to pass `repo_root=repo_root`, since the plan incorrectly claims the forwarding already exists — verify the updated call is `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)`.
evidence: Plan text: 'build_payload(verdict, *, material=None) in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py gains a repo_root=None keyword (its caller emit already holds repo_root and passes it through)'
sidecar.py:60: `payload = build_payload(verdict, material=material)` — repo_root NOT passed
sidecar.py:51: `def emit(verdict, *, material=None, repo_root=None)` — repo_root is present in emit's signature but not forwarded
findings.finding: `emit` does NOT currently pass `repo_root` to `build_payload`: the plan asserts 'its caller `emit` already holds repo_root and passes it through', but `sidecar.py:60` shows `build_payload(verdict, material=material)` — `repo_root` is absent. Adding `repo_root=None` to `build_payload`'s signature alone is insufficient; the call site inside `emit` must also be updated to `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)`, otherwise `verified_at_sha` and `regver` will always be stamped with `repo_root=None` and the git-HEAD fallback will silently use `None` as the path.
findings.location: What section — 'its caller emit already holds repo_root and passes it through'; sidecar.py:60
scenarios: Implementer adds `repo_root=None` to `build_payload` but does not update the `emit` call site at line 60; `build_payload` always receives `repo_root=None`, so `registry_version(None)` returns a packagewide stamp (not repo-specific) and `verified_at_sha` falls back to `git rev-parse HEAD` of the process cwd rather than the actual `repo_root`, silently producing wrong baseline values for BLOCK-loop re-reviews in multi-repo environments.
findings.suggested_fix: In `sidecar.py:60`, change `payload = build_payload(verdict, material=material)` to `payload = build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the `except Exception` at `attest.py:277` (the signing-error path) must ALSO fall through to the sidecar branch, not just the line 231 early return — add explicit guidance or a code sketch showing the restructured try/except boundary in the two-branch control flow.
evidence: Plan text: 'BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline — a signature verification error AND a successful verification whose manifest is not a plan-review manifest (the early return at attest.py:231) — now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible'
signing.py:620: `raise SigningError(f"Error: ticket '{ticket_id}' not found")` — verify_signature raises on unresolvable ticket
attest.py:277–281: `except Exception: ... return {"eligible": False, "reasons": reasons}` — currently returns ineligible, NOT falling through to any sidecar branch
attest.py:230–231: `if not is_plan_review_manifest(manifest): return {"eligible": False, "reasons": reasons}` — this is the only 'early return' named
findings.finding: The plan describes only the early return at `attest.py:231` as the path that will 'fall through to the sidecar branch', but `verify_signature` can also RAISE (a `SigningError` when the ticket cannot be resolved — `signing.py:620`), which currently hits the `except` at `attest.py:277` and returns `{eligible:False}` directly. The plan's two-branch restructuring must also handle this `except` path to fall through to the sidecar branch; the plan's text says both paths 'now fall through' but only names the line 231 early return as the structural target, leaving the `except` clause unaddressed.
findings.location: What section — 'BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline — a signature verification error AND a successful verification whose manifest is not a plan-review manifest (the early return at attest.py:231) — now fall through to the sidecar branch'; attest.py:277
scenarios: A ticket whose git tracker is temporarily unresolvable causes `verify_signature` to raise; the `except` clause returns ineligible immediately without consulting the sidecar — BLOCK loops on such tickets never benefit from the new fallback despite the plan's stated intent.

Implementer restructures only the line 231 branch into a sidecar fallback but leaves the `except` at line 277 as a hard-return, creating an inconsistency between the plan's stated semantics ('both paths fall through') and the implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py` tests that assert on `reasons['signed']` or the early-return behavior of the no-signature path (e.g. `test_no_signature_not_eligible`) as needing update to the new sidecar-branch reasons dict shape (`sidecar_baseline` replaces `signed`).
evidence: test_no_signature_not_eligible asserts `d['reasons']['signed'] is False` (line 88)
Sidecar branch reasons dict defined in plan as {sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window} — no `signed` key
Plan says 'BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline … now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible' — making the no-signed scenario a sidecar-branch result
ABSENCE: the plan's What/Scope/AC sections do not enumerate existing signature-negative test cases as requiring update
findings.finding: The plan reroutes the no-valid-signature path in `remediation_mode_candidate` to the sidecar branch, but does NOT enumerate that existing unit tests in `tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py` assert on the old signature-branch `reasons` key `signed` in that scenario — those tests will fail with a KeyError or incorrect assertion after the change.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py:84-88 (`test_no_signature_not_eligible`) and the What section ('BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline … now fall through to the sidecar branch')
scenarios: `python -m pytest tests/unit -k 'remediation' -x` fails on `test_no_signature_not_eligible` with KeyError on `reasons['signed']` because the sidecar branch emits a different reasons dict shape
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Scope IN item: 'update `test_no_signature_not_eligible` (and any other test asserting `reasons["signed"]` in the no-signature scenario) to assert on `reasons["sidecar_baseline"]` instead, and verify the sidecar-branch path.' Then add it to the AC matrix coverage in AC2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 'CREATE `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_block_loop_remediation.py`' item to the What section (or Scope IN list) so implementers know this is a new file, not an existing test to pass.
evidence: Directory listing of tests/interfaces/lifecycle/ does not include test_block_loop_remediation.py
Search for 'block_loop_remediation' returns no matches across the repo
ABSENCE: the plan's Scope or What section does not mention creating this file
findings.finding: The integration test `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_block_loop_remediation.py` referenced in AC4 does not exist in the repo — it is a new file the plan must create, but the plan does not explicitly state this in Scope or the What section.
findings.location: AC4 oracle: '`python -m pytest tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_block_loop_remediation.py -x` green'
scenarios: An implementer treats the AC4 oracle as a test to run against existing code, finds the file absent, and is uncertain whether to create it or search for an equivalent

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all consumers of the `remediation_mode_candidate` return dict (beyond `eligible`) — specifically `verdict['coverage']['remediation']` serialization into the sidecar — and confirm that the new `baseline` key and branch-conditional `reasons` shape are a documented, intentional sidecar schema additive change.
evidence: verdict['coverage']['remediation'] = remediation (line 518) persists the entire decision dict including new `baseline` and changed `reasons` shape into the sidecar
_maybe_apply_rising_floor checks `remediation.get('eligible')` only (line 186) — safe
The sidecar payload at build_payload captures `coverage` verbatim — the new `baseline` and reshaped `reasons` will appear in all future sidecars, changing the serialized shape
ABSENCE: no consumer audit of the decision dict's shape change is mentioned in What or Scope
findings.finding: The plan adds a `baseline: "signature" | "sidecar"` field to the dict returned by `remediation_mode_candidate`, but does not enumerate downstream consumers of that dict that read its shape — specifically `_maybe_apply_rising_floor` (which reads `remediation.get('eligible')`) and any telemetry/logging that serializes `reasons` — to confirm no consumer breaks on the new key.
findings.location: What section: 'the returned dict gains `baseline: "signature" | "sidecar"`'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:186 (`remediation.get('eligible')`), :518 (`verdict['coverage']['remediation'] = remediation`)
scenarios: Offline analysis tooling or downstream tests that parse `coverage.remediation.reasons.signed` from sidecar payloads receive a `KeyError` or `None` after the shape change

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exception-boundary structure for the two-branch rekey: state explicitly that the sidecar fallback branch is covered by the same outer catch-all `except Exception` (or an equivalent nested try), so a sidecar read error in the fallback still yields `{eligible: False}` rather than propagating — preserving the 'NEVER raises' docstring contract.
evidence: Plan: 'BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline - a signature verification error AND a successful verification whose manifest is not a plan-review manifest (the early return at attest.py:231) - now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible'
attest.py:222–225: 'NEVER raises (the docstring contract): the WHOLE body is guarded — a read error in ANY precondition ... leaves that precondition False'
attest.py:227–281: single outer try/except covering the entire body including both signature AND sidecar reads
ABSENCE: the plan contains no specification of how the exception-scope boundary is restructured to allow fall-through while preserving the outer catch-all
findings.finding: The plan requires that two current early-exit paths in remediation_mode_candidate — (a) a signing exception and (b) `not is_plan_review_manifest(manifest)` returning ineligible at attest.py:231 — now 'fall through to the sidecar branch,' but the existing outer try/except at attest.py:227–281 wraps the entire body as a single catch-all. The plan does not specify how this exception boundary is restructured: whether the sidecar-fallback code runs inside the same outer try (inheriting the fail-safe catch-all), whether the signature attempt gets its own inner try, or how a signing exception is distinguished from a sidecar exception. An implementer following 'two-branch control flow' literally could write the sidecar branch outside the outer try, causing a sidecar read error to propagate uncaught and violate the function's 'never raises' docstring contract.
findings.location: What section — 'Rekey eligibility in remediation_mode_candidate'; attest.py:227–281
scenarios: Implementer writes sidecar fallback outside the outer try → a sidecar read error (e.g., OSError on the ticket dir) propagates as an uncaught exception, crashing the plan review rather than degrading to ineligible.
Implementer wraps only the signing call in an inner try/except and falls through to the sidecar branch, but forgets to maintain the outer catch-all around the sidecar branch — same failure mode.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Rekey eligibility' spec: 'Both branches execute within the existing outer try/except catch-all (attest.py:277–281): the signature attempt is wrapped in an inner try/except that catches any exception and falls through to the sidecar branch; the sidecar branch executes within the outer try, so any sidecar read error is caught by the outer handler and returns ineligible.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update parent epic 7d43's What/Scope/ACs to explicitly authorize the sidecar-fallback eligibility path (sourcing verified_at_sha/regver/material_fingerprint from the prior REVIEW_RESULT sidecar as an alternative baseline when no valid plan-review manifest exists) before landing this leaf — the parent's current spec ties the code-unchanged baseline exclusively to 'prior manifest vs current run' and does not enumerate BLOCK-loop eligibility as in-scope.
evidence: Parent epic 7d43 AC: 'Remediation-mode gate: config key … code-unchanged precondition via verified_at_sha equality (prior manifest vs current run)'

Parent epic 7d43 gate description: 'plan changed, code unchanged since baseline, registry unchanged, a prior sidecar (with finding text) is available, and the last review … is within the window' — prior sidecar is a precondition, not a baseline source

Leaf What: 'SIDECAR FALLBACK only when no valid plan-review manifest exists — baseline = the most recent REVIEW_RESULT sidecar … code_unchanged compares the current run's code SHA against the sidecar's verified_at_sha'
Parent scope IN/OUT: no mention of sidecar-as-baseline or BLOCK-loop-eligibility as an in-scope mechanism; the parent's entire gate design flows through a signed manifest
findings.finding: The leaf introduces a sidecar-fallback eligibility path (sourcing verified_at_sha, regver, material_fingerprint baselines from the prior REVIEW_RESULT sidecar when no valid plan-review manifest exists) that the parent epic 7d43 does not authorize. The parent explicitly specifies 'code-unchanged precondition via verified_at_sha equality (prior manifest vs current run)' and treats the prior sidecar as a separate, independent precondition — not as an alternative baseline source. The parent's remediation-mode gate design is entirely premised on a valid certified signature; the leaf's two-branch control flow (signature path | sidecar fallback) extends the gate's triggering surface to BLOCK-loop scenarios the parent never enumerated in its scope, What, or ACs.
findings.location: What section — 'Rekey eligibility in remediation_mode_candidate with explicit two-branch control flow' and 'SIDECAR FALLBACK only when no valid plan-review manifest exists'
scenarios: A BLOCK verdict (no signature) triggers the sidecar fallback and marks the next review eligible for the rising floor — a capability the parent's gate design did not include and whose safety properties (trusting sidecar-stamped SHA over a signed manifest) the parent never reviewed.

If the parent is updated to authorize this path and the leaf is re-reviewed against the updated parent, the design is self-consistent; without that update the leaf diverges from the parent's contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to epic 7d43's Scope/IN and the remediation-mode gate AC: 'When no valid certified plan-review manifest exists (e.g. a BLOCK loop), the gate MAY use the most-recent REVIEW_RESULT sidecar as the baseline source for verified_at_sha, regver, and material_fingerprint, provided the sidecar carries all three fields (fail-safe: any missing field → ineligible).' Then re-review the parent before proceeding with the leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `verified_at_sha` fallback sentinel: either reuse `signing.current_head_sha()` and explicitly filter its `'unknown'` return to `None` before stamping the sidecar (so the plan's `None`-fails-precondition contract holds), or document why a new inline subprocess call is preferred over the existing helper.
evidence: Plan: 'git-HEAD fallback when it is None (local-mode reads): the committed HEAD SHA of repo_root, best-effort subprocess, None only if that also fails'
signing.py:525-541: `current_head_sha` returns `'unknown'` (a sentinel string) on subprocess failure or OSError — never `None`
Plan's sidecar-branch code_unchanged check: 'None on either side fails the precondition' — but `'unknown'` is not `None` and would pass this guard
ABSENCE: the plan never references `signing.current_head_sha` despite it being the existing implementation of this exact operation
findings.finding: The plan's git-HEAD subprocess fallback for `verified_at_sha` (stamped in `build_payload`) reinvents `signing.current_head_sha(repo_root)`, which already implements this exact logic (signing.py:525-541). Critically, `signing.current_head_sha` returns the sentinel string `'unknown'` on failure, not `None`. The plan specifies the fallback returns `None` only if the subprocess also fails. If the implementation reuses the existing helper without filtering `'unknown'`, two consecutive local-mode BLOCK reviews would both store `'unknown'` in their sidecars, and the sidecar-branch `code_unchanged` check (`sidecar.verified_at_sha == current_sha`) would compare `'unknown' == 'unknown'` and return `True` — producing a false `code_unchanged` signal in exactly the local-mode BLOCK loop the feature is designed to fix. The plan does not mention `signing.current_head_sha` or address this sentinel discrepancy.
findings.location: What section — 'Persist the baseline signals in the sidecar' / `build_payload` git-HEAD fallback specification
scenarios: Two consecutive local-mode BLOCK reviews (the 76%-of-extra-rounds case this plan targets): both calls to the git-HEAD fallback return `'unknown'`; the sidecar stores `'unknown'` for `verified_at_sha`; the eligibility check sees `'unknown' == 'unknown'` and concludes `code_unchanged=True` even though the code may have drifted between the two reviews.
findings.suggested_fix: In `build_payload`'s git-HEAD fallback, call `signing.current_head_sha(repo_root)` and then apply `sha if sha != 'unknown' else None` before stamping `verified_at_sha`. This reuses the existing tested helper and correctly maps the `'unknown'` sentinel to `None`, satisfying the plan's 'None only if that also fails' contract without reinventing the subprocess call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback-safety assertion to the plan stating that old code reading new-format sidecars (carrying `verified_at_sha`/`regver`) degrades gracefully — either by confirming the sidecar reader uses permissive dict-key access (not strict schema validation) so unknown fields are silently ignored, or by documenting a compensating-forward procedure if strict deserialization is used.
evidence: "Both fields are additive-optional on the existing plan-review sidecar schema; older sidecars without them simply fail the fallback preconditions (fail-safe)." — this sentence covers old-sidecar → new-code compatibility only
ABSENCE: the plan contains no assertion about new-sidecar → old-code compatibility (the rollback direction)
T11 POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE requirement: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
findings.finding: The plan addresses how NEW code reads OLD sidecars (additive-optional, fail-safe), but does NOT assert the schema state after a rollback — specifically, whether OLD code can safely read NEW sidecars that carry `verified_at_sha` and `regver`. If old code performs strict schema validation or structured deserialization of the sidecar, it may raise on unexpected fields, breaking the review gate for any sidecar written during the new-code window.
findings.location: What section — sidecar field stamping; also Acceptance Criteria (no rollback assertion present)
scenarios: Operator deploys new code; several BLOCK-loop reviews execute, stamping sidecars with the two new fields; operator rolls back to prior code version; old code encounters those new-format sidecars and either raises on deserialization or silently misreads the sidecar, potentially rendering the review gate broken for affected review sessions.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rollback note to the What section: 'Rollback safety (new-sidecar → old-code): the sidecar reader accesses fields by dict key; unknown keys are ignored, so old code encountering a new-format sidecar falls through to the signature path unchanged — no data loss and no gate breakage on rollback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact git invocation for the HEAD-SHA fallback in `build_payload` (command, working-directory argument, exception scope), and confirm that `repo_root=None` skips the fallback entirely rather than falling through to a CWD-relative git call.
evidence: Plan quote: 'git-HEAD fallback when it is None (local-mode reads): the committed HEAD SHA of repo_root, best-effort subprocess, None only if that also fails'
sidecar.py:358 `build_payload` is currently a pure function — no subprocess, no I/O

sidecar.py:95 `prune()` uses `subprocess.run(['git', '-C', tracker, 'rm', ...], check=True)` — pattern exists but differs in error-handling posture (`check=True` raises; the new fallback must swallow)
Partially-verified: the subprocess git pattern is established in the module but the new invocation's `repo_root=None` guard, exact command, and exception scope are unspecified
findings.finding: The plan introduces a subprocess git-HEAD SHA read inside `build_payload` (a currently pure, I/O-free function), but does not specify the exact git invocation — specifically how `repo_root=None` is handled (skip the fallback entirely? use CWD?), what git command and arguments are used, and whether failures (non-git directory, git not on PATH) are swallowed or propagated before the outer `emit` try/except catches them.
findings.location: What section — `build_payload(verdict, *, material=None)` gains `repo_root=None`; 'git-HEAD fallback when it is None (local-mode reads): the committed HEAD SHA of repo_root, best-effort subprocess, None only if that also fails'
scenarios: repo_root=None is passed (local mode without a known root) — does the fallback skip entirely or call git in CWD?
repo_root points to a non-git directory (e.g., a temp checkout) — git fails with CalledProcessError; is this caught before it escapes build_payload's scope?
git is unavailable on PATH — FileNotFoundError from subprocess.run; same question
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section: 'When repo_root is None the git fallback is skipped (returns None immediately); when repo_root is non-None, the fallback runs `subprocess.run(["git", "-C", repo_root, "rev-parse", "HEAD"], capture_output=True, check=True, text=True)` inside a try/except Exception that returns None on any failure — matching build_payload's existing never-raise contract.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the git-HEAD fallback MUST return `None` (not a sentinel string like `'unknown'`) on subprocess failure, and that implementors MUST NOT use `signing.head_sha` directly (which returns `'unknown'`) without normalizing the result — add this normalization requirement explicitly to the `build_payload` stamping rule.
evidence: Plan: 'git-HEAD fallback when it is None (local-mode reads): the committed HEAD SHA of repo_root, best-effort subprocess, None only if that also fails'
signing.py:524-541: `head_sha(repo_root)` returns `'unknown'` (not None) on subprocess failure: `return sha if out.returncode == 0 and sha else 'unknown'`

Plan eligibility rule: 'None on either side fails the precondition' — but `'unknown' != None`, so this precondition would NOT fire on an `'unknown'`/`'unknown'` pair, incorrectly treating it as code_unchanged=True
findings.finding: The git-HEAD fallback for `verified_at_sha` in `build_payload` says to stamp `None` on subprocess failure, but the existing `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` — the natural reuse point — returns the string sentinel `'unknown'` (not `None`) on failure. If an implementor calls `signing.head_sha` without normalizing `'unknown'`→`None`, the eligibility reader's precondition (`None on either side fails the precondition`) silently passes with a non-comparable sentinel string, potentially granting `code_unchanged=True` on an unresolvable HEAD.
findings.location: What section — `build_payload` new `repo_root` kwarg / `verified_at_sha` stamping rule
scenarios: A local-mode BLOCK loop where git is unavailable: `current_code_sha()` returns None, git-HEAD fallback also fails — `signing.head_sha` returns `'unknown'`, which is non-None. The sidecar stamps `'unknown'`. The eligibility reader compares `'unknown' == 'unknown'` → `code_unchanged=True`. The floor activates even though no reliable SHA baseline exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'The git-HEAD fallback calls `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` and maps its `"unknown"` sentinel to `None` before stamping — so a truly unresolvable HEAD produces `verified_at_sha: None`, which the eligibility reader treats as a missing baseline (precondition False).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the initial `reasons` dict is constructed in the refactored two-branch `remediation_mode_candidate`: state explicitly whether each branch initializes its own reasons dict (and what its zero-value shape is), and whether the outer `except` handler returns a branch-appropriate shape or a unified safe default.
evidence: Plan: 'the sidecar branch's reasons dict contains EXACTLY five keys — {sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window} — where `sidecar_baseline` is True when a prior sidecar carrying all three baseline fields exists; there is no `signed` and no separate `prior_sidecar` key in that branch'
Plan: 'the signature branch keeps today's keys verbatim ({signed, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, prior_sidecar, within_window})'

attest.py:214-221: current implementation initializes one `reasons` dict at the top (6-key signature shape) and the except clause returns it — the plan gives no instruction on how to reconcile this with two-branch key sets in the refactored version
ABSENCE: the plan never says 'initialize two separate reasons dicts, one per branch, before the try block' or 'the except clause returns a branch-appropriate empty reasons dict'
findings.finding: The refactored `remediation_mode_candidate` has two branches with incompatible `reasons` key sets (signature: 6 keys including `signed`/`prior_sidecar`; sidecar: 5 keys without them), but the plan never specifies the shape of the initial `reasons` dict that the outer `except`-clause fallback returns. Currently the function initializes `reasons` once at the top as the 6-key signature shape and the except handler returns it. After the refactor, if the sidecar branch raises mid-execution, the except handler returns a 6-key dict (with `signed`/`prior_sidecar`) even though the caller is on the sidecar branch — and callers asserting sidecar-branch key presence (e.g., AC2: `assert d["reasons"]["sidecar_baseline"] is False`) would KeyError.
findings.location: What section — two-branch eligibility rekey / `remediation_mode_candidate` refactor; also AC1 and AC2 oracle assertions
scenarios: Implementor retains single top-level reasons dict (the natural migration path). Sidecar branch raises after partial execution. Except handler returns a dict with `signed=False` / `prior_sidecar=False` keys. AC2 test asserts `d['reasons']['sidecar_baseline'] is False` → KeyError.
Implementor initializes `reasons={}` and only populates it inside each branch. The except handler returns `{}` → `eligible=all({}.values())=True` — accidentally eligible on error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether the sidecar schema string must remain `"plan_review_result_v2"` (additive, no bump), and if any version bump is permissible, require that `latest_review_result`'s schema guard be updated in lockstep — co-locate this constraint with the `build_payload` change description.
evidence: Plan: 'Both fields are additive-optional on the existing plan-review sidecar schema; older sidecars without them simply fail the fallback preconditions (fail-safe)'
sidecar.py:428: `build_payload` currently stamps `"schema": "plan_review_result_v2"`
sidecar.py:162-165: `latest_review_result` schema guard: `payload.get('schema') in ('plan_review_result_v1', 'plan_review_result_v2')` — a v3 bump silently breaks the reader
ABSENCE: plan never says 'do not bump the schema version' or 'if you bump, update the schema guard in latest_review_result'
findings.finding: The plan adds `verified_at_sha` and `regver` as additive fields to `build_payload` (which currently emits `"schema": "plan_review_result_v2"`) but never confirms the schema version string stays `"plan_review_result_v2"`. The `latest_review_result` schema guard ONLY accepts `"plan_review_result_v1"` or `"plan_review_result_v2"`; if an implementor bumps the schema to `v3` to distinguish old v2 (without the new fields) from new ones, ALL new sidecars would be rejected by the schema guard as 'foreign schema,' making the sidecar fallback branch permanently inoperative with no error.
findings.location: What section — `build_payload` new fields / schema version; also `latest_review_result` schema guard in sidecar.py:162-165
scenarios: Implementor reasons that new fields deserve a v3 bump for forward-compat. latest_review_result silently skips all v3 sidecars. The sidecar branch never finds a usable baseline. BLOCK loops remain permanently ineligible. The integration test (AC4) would catch this if it asserts `coverage.remediation.eligible` — but only if the test is run; no static check prevents the schema-guard/version mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'The schema string remains `"plan_review_result_v2"` — no version bump. The new fields are purely additive; `latest_review_result`'s schema guard (`v1`/`v2`) does not need updating.'

findings.finding: The plan adds a sidecar fallback branch where `reasons` omits the `signed` and `prior_sidecar` keys (present only in the signature branch), but `tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py` already contains tests that assert on those exact keys (e.g. `test_no_signature_not_eligible` asserts `d['reasons']['signed'] is False` at line 88; `test_no_prior_sidecar_findings_not_eligible` asserts `d['reasons']['prior_sidecar'] is False` at lines 127/130) and are NOT enumerated as edit targets in the plan's Scope or What section.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py:84-131 / What section (two-branch control flow)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Scope section: 'Update `test_no_signature_not_eligible` to cover two sub-cases: (a) no signature AND no valid sidecar → ineligible via sidecar branch; (b) no signature BUT valid sidecar → eligible via sidecar branch. Update `test_no_prior_sidecar_findings_not_eligible` to assert against `sidecar_baseline` (not `prior_sidecar`) when in the sidecar branch. Add a test for the new `baseline: "signature" | "sidecar"` key in the returned dict.'

findings.finding: The sidecar-branch `code_unchanged` precondition fails permanently for local-mode BLOCK loops (both the prior sidecar's `verified_at_sha` and `current_code_sha()` are None in local mode, and the plan states 'None on either side fails the precondition'), making the sidecar fallback structurally inert for local-mode users — the same structural inertness the plan is trying to fix for BLOCK loops generally.
findings.location: What section / 'Rekey eligibility in remediation_mode_candidate' paragraph — 'code_unchanged compares the current run's code SHA (current_code_sha(), argument-less) against the sidecar's verified_at_sha — None on either side fails the precondition'

findings.finding: The existing unit test `test_no_signature_not_eligible` (and the sidecar-branch fall-through logic for the `signed=False` path) will break under the new two-branch design: it asserts `d['reasons']['signed'] is False`, but the sidecar branch emits NO `signed` key — the assertion will KeyError or silently mismatch.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py:84-88
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `## Scope` IN list: 'update tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py: revise tests that assert on reasons["signed"] for the signed=False path to instead assert on the sidecar-branch reasons shape.'

findings.finding: The plan incorrectly asserts that 'a signature verification error' hits 'the early return at attest.py:231.' In the actual code, a signing *exception* (e.g., from `signing.verify_signature`) is caught by the outer `except Exception` at attest.py:277 and returns ineligible from line 281, never reaching line 231. Only a *non-raising but failed* verification (verified=False) — plus a wrong manifest type — hits line 231. The plan's proposed 'fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible' must handle BOTH exit points (line 231 and line 281), but the plan describes only one. An implementation that only redirects the line-231 exit will silently leave signing-exception cases returning ineligible without consulting the sidecar.
findings.location: What section: 'BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline - a signature verification error AND a successful verification whose manifest is not a plan-review manifest (the early return at attest.py:231) - now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the 'What' section to distinguish the two paths: (a) for the non-raising, non-plan-review-manifest case (line 231 exit), remove the early return and fall through to the sidecar branch; (b) for the signing-exception case (line 277-281 exit), restructure the try/except — e.g. catch the signing call in an inner try and fall through to the sidecar branch on exception, rather than the outer catch returning ineligible immediately.

findings.finding: The plan says two non-exception cases (verification error, non-plan-review manifest) now fall through to the sidecar branch, but never specifies whether a *raised exception* from `verify_signature` (or any signature-path code) also triggers the sidecar fallback. The current outer `try/except Exception` at attest.py:277 catches all exceptions and returns ineligible before the sidecar branch can run. Restructuring for two-branch control flow requires explicit re-scoping of the exception perimeter, but the plan leaves this undefined.
findings.location: What section — two-branch control flow paragraph; attest.py:227-281
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'An exception raised by `verify_signature` itself is caught by the outer fail-safe (ineligible), NOT routed to the sidecar branch — only the two result-based cases (verified=False and non-plan-review manifest) fall through.' Then ensure the two-branch implementation uses an inner try scoped to the signature path for the fall-through cases while keeping the outer try for the sidecar path.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
